questions.q: What distinguishes intrinsic semiconductors from doped semiconductors?
options: Intrinsic semiconductors are always metals
Intrinsic semiconductors have no intentionally added impurities; doped ones contain controlled amounts of dopants
Intrinsic semiconductors conduct better than doped ones
Doped semiconductors are always silicon
questions.explanation: Intrinsic semiconductors like pure silicon depend on thermal excitation for conductivity. Doped semiconductors have dopants (donors or acceptors) added intentionally to dramatically increase conductivity at specified carrier type.

questions.q: In n-type doping, what does the dopant element do?
options: It accepts electrons to create holes
It donates extra electrons to conduct current
It fills band gaps
It prevents electron flow
questions.explanation: N-type (negative) dopants like phosphorus added to silicon donate extra electrons. These electrons become mobile charge carriers. The dopant atoms substitute for silicon in the lattice with one extra electron.

questions.q: How does the band gap of a semiconductor relate to its electrical properties at room temperature?
options: Larger band gap means higher conductivity
Smaller band gap makes it easier for thermal energy to excite electrons to conduction band, increasing conductivity
Band gap has no effect on properties
Band gap only matters at very low temperatures
questions.explanation: Band gap determines the minimum energy needed to create mobile electrons. Smaller gaps (like germanium at 0.66 eV vs silicon at 1.1 eV) require less thermal energy, resulting in higher intrinsic conductivity at the same temperature.

questions.q: A p-n junction is formed by joining p-type and n-type silicon. What occurs at the junction in thermal equilibrium?
options: Current flows indefinitely
A depletion region forms as electrons diffuse from n to p side and holes from p to n side, creating an electric field that stops further diffusion
The two regions remain electrically neutral
Ions immediately recombine into neutral atoms
questions.explanation: At equilibrium, diffusion of majority carriers (electrons from n-side, holes from p-side) creates a space-charge region and built-in electric field that opposes further diffusion. This depletion region is crucial for diode function.

questions.q: Forward bias on a p-n diode means applying voltage such that the external field opposes the built-in field. What is the result?
options: The depletion region widens and resistance increases
The depletion region narrows, reducing barrier height and allowing significant current flow
Current remains zero regardless of voltage
The device acts as an open circuit
questions.explanation: Reducing the effective barrier allows majority carriers to overcome it. Forward bias decreases depletion width and potential barrier, enabling exponential current increase as given by the Shockley diode equation.

questions.q: The saturation current Is in a p-n diode depends strongly on which factor?
options: Applied voltage
Temperature (increasing exponentially with temperature)
Doping level
Light exposure
questions.explanation: Is increases exponentially with temperature because it depends on intrinsic carrier concentration ni, which exponentially increases with temperature. This is why semiconductor devices are temperature-sensitive.

questions.q: In a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor field-effect transistor (MOSFET), what is the function of the gate voltage?
options: It provides the main current pathway
It modulates the conductivity of the channel by controlling the electric field and hence the number of charge carriers
It supplies power to the device
It operates the same as a BJT base
questions.explanation: Gate voltage creates an electric field through the insulating oxide that affects carrier concentration in the channel beneath. This field effect modulates conductivity without significant gate current, unlike BJTs.

questions.q: Why is silicon the dominant semiconductor material in industry rather than germanium, despite germanium's smaller band gap?
options: Silicon is cheaper to mine
Silicon has better thermal stability due to larger band gap and vastly superior oxide (SiO₂) for insulation and device fabrication
Germanium only conducts at cryogenic temperatures
Silicon has higher electron mobility
questions.explanation: Although germanium has higher intrinsic conductivity due to smaller band gap, silicon's larger gap (1.1 vs 0.66 eV) means lower leakage at high temperature. Critically, SiO₂ is an excellent insulator and forms naturally, making silicon ideal for integrated circuits.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a heterojunction in semiconductor devices?
options: To increase mechanical strength
To create a junction between two different semiconductor materials, often with different band gaps, enabling better control of carrier transport and wavelength
To reduce device cost
To eliminate the need for doping
questions.explanation: Heterojunctions combine materials with different band gaps and lattice parameters to engineer device properties. They're used in high-efficiency LEDs, lasers, and high-speed transistors to improve carrier confinement and performance.

questions.q: The Miller indices notation (100) and (111) in silicon represent different crystal planes. Why do growth and etching rates differ on these planes?
options: Crystal planes have different colors
Different atomic densities and bonding configurations on different planes lead to different reaction kinetics
All planes have identical growth rates
Crystal planes are only relevant for optical properties
questions.explanation: Atomic density, bond orientation, and defect structure vary across crystallographic planes. High-density planes like (111) etch more slowly because more bonds must break. This anisotropy is exploited in microfabrication.
